region_of_origin: India (Punjab / North India); served in Singapore Indian-Mexican fusion restaurants

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to tandoori-marinated grilled chicken exists; A00LC (chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of half tandoori chicken weighs approximately 400 g edible portion, yielding roughly 680 kcal; a full chicken serving is approximately 800 g.

nutrient_source.title: Tandoori Chicken, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), Table: Poultry and Egg Products
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN 2017 reports tandoori chicken (cooked, bone-in) per 100 g edible portion. Values used directly. Sodium estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry 'Chicken, tandoori, cooked' (FDC ID 2103689) as ICMR-NIN does not report sodium; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for tandoori chicken exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked chicken preparations (grilled chicken, GI ~0–15) from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Carbohydrate content is 1.5 g per 100 g; GL per 400 g serving = (15 × 6.0) / 100 = 0.9, which is negligible. GI category: low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~1280 mg per half-chicken serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to a quarter chicken and request reduced-salt marinade, as a half-chicken serving contributes approximately 1280 mg sodium, representing over 55% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.5 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tandoori chicken is a suitable protein-rich, low-GI food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its negligible carbohydrate content (approximately 6 g per half-chicken serving) produces a glycemic load of less than 1, posing minimal postprandial glucose impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2 g per 100 g) and sodium (~320 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tandoori chicken provides a relatively lean protein source with moderate saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose skinless preparations to reduce saturated fat intake and be mindful of the sodium contribution from the spiced marinade.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori chicken is a classic North Indian dish originating from the Punjab region, prepared by marinating bone-in chicken pieces in a mixture of plain yoghurt, lemon juice, garlic, ginger, and a blend of aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli powder, and garam masala. The marinated chicken is traditionally cooked at very high temperatures (above 400°C) in a cylindrical clay oven called a tandoor, which imparts a characteristic smoky, charred exterior while keeping the interior moist and tender. The dish is typically served with sliced onions, lemon wedges, mint chutney, and naan bread. In Singapore, tandoori chicken is commonly available at Indian restaurants and Indian-Mexican fusion establishments. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food with approximately 25 g protein and only 1.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it suitable for low-glycaemic and high-protein dietary patterns. It is naturally gluten-free and halal. The sodium content from the marinade (~320 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is very low (~15), and the glycemic load per half-chicken serving is negligible (<1), making it an excellent choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is rich in B vitamins, particularly niacin (B3) and B6, which support energy metabolism.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tandoori chicken?
Is tandoori chicken good for diabetics?
Is tandoori chicken high in protein?
How much sodium in tandoori chicken?
Tandoori chicken half vs full calories
Can I eat tandoori chicken on a low carb diet?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN 2017 (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods), which is the primary approved regional source for Indian dishes. Sodium supplemented from USDA FoodData Central as ICMR-NIN does not report sodium for this entry. GI estimated from analogous grilled chicken data in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables (University of Sydney GI Database affiliated). Ontology anchored to FoodOn FOODON_03302051 (exact match) and SNOMED CT 226803008 (exact match).
notes: 1. Cuisine field corrected from 'Mexican' (input error) to 'Indian'; the dish is a canonical North Indian preparation. The serving restaurants are Indian-Mexican fusion establishments in Singapore, which explains the erroneous cuisine tag in the seed record. 2. Sodium (320 mg per 100 g) is sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 2103689 ('Chicken, tandoori, cooked') as ICMR-NIN 2017 does not report sodium for this entry; this is an approved secondary source. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.8), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.4), trans_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (290), calcium_mg (18), iron_mg (1.2), cholesterol_mg (85), vitamin_c_mg (1.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (18), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.08), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.15), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (8.5), vitamin_b6_mg (0.55), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.35), folate_mcg_dfe (8.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.2), vitamin_e_mg (0.5), phosphorus_mg (210), magnesium_mg (26), zinc_mg (1.8), selenium_mcg (18), water_g (65) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 2103689 as supplementary micronutrient data. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on grilled/cooked chicken preparations from Atkinson et al. 2021; no direct tandoori chicken GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. FoodEx2 code A00LC is the closest parent (chicken meat, cooked); no tandoori-specific FoodEx2 code exists. 6. Multilingual names provided for Indian-origin dish served in Singapore context: Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi. 7. Price data from input (SGD 21.00) retained as contextual metadata but not included in RESULT template fields as the template does not support price fields.
